behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "signature does not match ... 401 ... body is not forwarded." Slot B: handoff boundary (HTTP handler -> queue writer). Slot C: If lowered to Unit, a pure signature-check test could pass while the real handler still calls enqueue on failure. Integration-brake: no real OS/network/subprocess required; no AI/ML output; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejection-gate: System rejected because behavior does not require real network/kernel effects, only module wiring and side-effect suppression.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with an invalid `X-Signature-256` and a spy/fake queue module; assert HTTP status 401 and zero enqueue calls for that request body.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "written to the persistent queue before ... 200 acknowledgment." Slot B: handoff/ordering boundary between queue write completion and HTTP response. Slot C: If lowered to Unit, ordering bugs across modules (responding before write resolves) can be missed. Integration-brake: no required real OS/network/subprocess; no AI/ML output; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejection-gate: System rejected because the failure is ordering in local async wiring, reproducible with in-process queue implementation/fake persistence.
behaviors.test_description: Use a controllable queue adapter promise; verify handler does not send 200 until queue `write` resolves, and does send 200 immediately after successful write.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing ... to finish." Slot B: handoff boundary (enqueue/dispatch vs async worker completion). Slot C: If lowered to Unit, you can miss promise-chaining mistakes where handler awaits processor completion indirectly. Integration-brake: no required real OS/network/subprocess; no AI/ML output; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejection-gate: System rejected because real external infra is not required to expose await-vs-fire-and-forget bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + a deliberately slow downstream processor; assert response is emitted before processor completion signal while event is still accepted for async work.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "within 3 seconds wall-clock ... when ... earlier events is slow and concurrent." Slot B: real-infra/time/concurrency boundary (actual HTTP server/event loop scheduling under concurrent load). Slot C: If lowered to Integration, mocked timing and in-process shortcuts can hide queue starvation/backpressure issues that breach 3s in real runtime. Rejection-gate: Workflow/Agentic rejected because no AI model decisions or multi-agent steps are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service process and issue concurrent HTTP webhook requests while earlier events are intentionally slow; measure end-to-end wall-clock latency and assert each response is <3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "ID was already received is not written ... a second time." Slot B: state handoff boundary across requests (dedupe state/persistence + handler). Slot C: If lowered to Unit, single-call logic can pass while duplicate suppression fails with real shared state across two requests. Integration-brake: no required real OS/network/subprocess; no AI/ML output; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejection-gate: System rejected because duplicate detection can be validated with local module wiring and persistent/local store semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Send two webhook requests with the same event ID through the handler against the real dedupe/queue modules; assert only one queue write is persisted.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "queue reports it is full ... returns 503 with Retry-After." Slot B: handoff boundary from queue error/status to HTTP mapping. Slot C: If lowered to Unit, mapping may look correct in isolation but fail when real handler error propagation middleware is wired. Integration-brake: no required real OS/network/subprocess; no AI/ML output; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejection-gate: System rejected because this behavior is status translation, reproducible without real network/kernel dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue module to return a deterministic 'full' condition; invoke handler and assert HTTP 503 plus correct `Retry-After` header and no success ack.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "persistent queue actually enforces ... capacity limit and rejects events." Slot B: real-infra-within-app boundary (queue module + real local persistence behavior, not a mock). Slot C: If lowered to Unit, mocked storage can always 'accept' writes and miss true limit enforcement defects. Integration-brake: no required external network/subprocess; no AI/ML output; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejection-gate: System considered but rejected because capacity enforcement can be validated against in-process queue + local DB/filesystem backend without OS-level distributed effects.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend with a small configured limit, enqueue until full, then assert the next enqueue is rejected with the queue's overflow signal.
